My hooves are slipping on the mudslides, making it hard to keep my balance.
I must carry the injured villagers on my back to reach the high ground.
The rushing river is too deep for me to ford without risking being swept away.
I can smell the smoke from the forest fires drifting across the valley floor.
My four legs allow me to gallop through the debris faster than any man.
My hooves strike the mountain stone in a frantic rhythm as the sky splits open.
I shift my weight and toss my head, smelling ozone and sulfur on the wind.
The stars are drifting from their fixed paths, leaving me blind to the seasonal maps.
I must gallop toward the valley floor before the shimmering light scorches the high grass.
My equine half trembles with a primal fear that my human mind cannot explain.
These markers are placed too close to the grazing meadows where my herd feeds.
My hooves have trodden this soil since the great migrations, long before your fences.
You cannot expect me to negotiate while you block the path to the river.
I will not allow your cattle to trample the sacred groves of the forest.
Move your boundary stones back ten paces or we shall settle this with bows.
My hooves strike the stone with a hollow ring as I approach the swirling light.
I shift my weight onto my haunches, wary of how this rift might affect my four legs.
The scent of ozone fills my nostrils, masking the familiar smell of the pine forest.
I reach out with my human hand to touch the shimmering edge of the gateway.
If the terrain on the other side is rocky, my gait will be slow and labored.
My hooves sink deep into the damp loam, making every step a heavy struggle against the sucking mud.
I must keep my human torso low and steady to avoid snapping the thick canopy branches overhead.
The scent of crushed ferns and wet earth fills my nostrils, masking the trail I sought to follow.
These narrow gaps between the ancient oaks are far too tight for my broad equine flanks to pass.
I can feel the vibration of something large moving through the underbrush long before I see it.
My hooves have grown restless against the packed earth while the woods remain deathly still.
The wind has forgotten how to whisper, leaving only the heavy thrum of my own heart.
I shift my weight from side to side, aching for the sound of a distant gallop.
Not a single bird calls from the canopy to break this suffocating, timeless void of noise.
I listen with every fiber of my being, but the horizon offers nothing but a hollow peace.
My hooves strike the hard earth as I wheel around to face the enemy.
I will drive my spear through your chest before you can draw another breath.
Step back or feel the thunder of my gallop crashing directly into your line.
You think your steel blade can match the strength of my four charging legs.
I can outrun any archer while raining arrows down upon your fleeing, terrified ranks.
This silver-plated grooming brush is the only tool that reaches the deep knots in my flank.
I keep this heavy leather saddle-bag locked away because it holds the rarest salts for my hooves.
No one may touch this ancestral bow, carved from the heartwood of a tree that saw my forebears.
This wide, reinforced golden collar is my most precious prize, reflecting the sun as I gallop across the plains.
I cherish this oversized silk blanket that keeps my equine half warm during the freezing mountain nights.
This valley's grass is thinning fast, and my herd cannot survive another winter here.
I must carefully pace my grazing so the meadow has time to regrow before the frost.
My hooves leave deep ruts in the drying mud as I search for a hidden spring.
We must divide the remaining salt licks among the foals first to ensure their growth.
I can feel the water level dropping in the creek, leaving us far too little for the week.
